  • Hide All Code

HUGEN 2072 Project 8

Instructions

Below is a guided tutorial to perform some advanced analyses building upon the results from project 7’s GWAS of height. Some code has been provided to you and some you will need to write yourself.

Project 8 has three components:

  • Scripting/coding: read through this file and complete the pipeline by contributing your code where indicated.

  • Narrative: knit the completed .Rmd to a code-integrated HTML report of your QC process and decisions.

    • Your report must answer all the questions and perform all the tasks indicated in the instructions/prompts given for each step.

    • You must submit a successfully-knitted HTML file and the .Rmd in order to earn a passing grade for this project.

    • Set up your report to flow like an actual document you might show your supervisor to summarize the project, emphasizing what was done at each step rather than the code used to do it

    • Submit your .Rmd and HTML to Canvas

  • Presentation: you will make a 10-min (maximum) recording of yourself with Panopto in which you summarize this GWAS. Instructions will given on Canvas.

In each part of this project you will implement one analysis strategy we learned in class (some steps completed for you).

Since the code below is incomplete, some of the R code chunks are set to eval=FALSE so that knitting to HTML will succeed. You will need to change them to eval=TRUE as you progress so that your knitted document shows the results of running each chunk.

It is expected that you will copy this file somewhere to your directory on the CRC cluster so that you can use the RStudio Server, where all the packages you need have been installed already.

To standardize the starting point of this project, I have provided the necessary results from project 7, so please use those instead of any of your generated files.

Part 0 - Setup Workspace and Load Sample Data

First, load the packages we’ll be using.

# Load the required packages for the project (Install them if necessary)

library("SeqArray", quietly = TRUE)
library("SeqVarTools", quietly = TRUE)
library("GENESIS", quietly = TRUE)
library("locuszoomr", quietly = TRUE)
library("EnsDb.Hsapiens.v86", quietly = TRUE)
library("rsq", quietly = TRUE)
library("EasyQC", quietly = TRUE)
library("qqman", quietly = TRUE)
library("tidyverse", quietly = TRUE)
library("ggforce", quietly = TRUE)


# Close any open GDS files before proceeding (think of this as starting off with a 'blank slate')

showfile.gds(closeall = T, verbose = T)

All of the files you will need are located on the cluster in /ix1/hugen2072_2025s/p8/.

# path to folder for project
shared_folder <- "/ix1/hugen2072-2026s/p8/"

Part 1 - Evaluate GWAS hits

A version of the GWAS from project 7 has been provided for you in the file p8_study1_gwas_results.txt.

The columns here are:

  • variant.id: the variant ID from the GDS file

  • chr: chromosome

  • pos: position (hg38)

  • effect_allele: allele coded additively for GWAS

  • other_allele: the other allele at the variant (non-effect allele)

  • imp_r2: imputation quality r-squared

  • freq: the frequency of the effect allele

  • genotyped: TRUE if variant was genotyped, FALSE if it was imputed

  • n.obs: sample size with non-missing data at that variant

  • MAC: minor allele count

  • Score, Score.SE, Score.Stat, Score.pval, Est, Est.SE, and PVE: columns from assocTestSingle() run.

  • strand: strand indicator

# read in results from GWAS 1
gwas1 <- read_table(paste0(shared_folder, "p8_study1_gwas_results.txt"))
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   variant.id = col_double(),
##   chr = col_double(),
##   pos = col_double(),
##   effect_allele = col_character(),
##   other_allele = col_character(),
##   imp_r2 = col_double(),
##   freq = col_double(),
##   genotyped = col_logical(),
##   n.obs = col_double(),
##   MAC = col_double(),
##   Score = col_double(),
##   Score.SE = col_double(),
##   Score.Stat = col_double(),
##   Score.pval = col_double(),
##   Est = col_double(),
##   Est.SE = col_double(),
##   PVE = col_double(),
##   strand = col_character()
## )
gwas1
## # A tibble: 104,466 × 18
##    variant.id   chr     pos effect_allele other_allele imp_r2   freq genotyped
##         <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>         <chr>         <dbl>  <dbl> <lgl>    
##  1         21     1  891887 T             C             0.985 0.895  FALSE    
##  2         28     1  904493 T             C             1     0.356  FALSE    
##  3         30     1  911085 T             C             0.990 0.195  FALSE    
##  4         54     1  993941 C             A             0.998 0.934  FALSE    
##  5         70     1 1027340 T             C             0.993 0.0974 FALSE    
##  6         73     1 1035075 A             G             0.995 0.0110 FALSE    
##  7         74     1 1036806 T             C             0.981 0.0129 FALSE    
##  8         80     1 1059011 T             G             0.998 0.773  FALSE    
##  9         88     1 1086657 T             C             0.992 0.588  FALSE    
## 10        114     1 1194540 A             G             0.999 0.104  FALSE    
## # ℹ 104,456 more rows
## # ℹ 10 more variables: n.obs <dbl>, MAC <dbl>, Score <dbl>, Score.SE <dbl>,
## #   Score.Stat <dbl>, Score.pval <dbl>, Est <dbl>, Est.SE <dbl>, PVE <dbl>,
## #   strand <chr>

Part 1 - Report

Top SNP

In the results file provided to you, which SNP has the most significant association with height? Report the GDS variant.id, chromosome, position, alleles, GWAS p-value, GWAS effect size, effect allele, effect allele frequency, and whether the SNP was genotyped or imputed.

Variant ID: 542766

Chr: 19

Pos: 28492903

Other Allele: C

Effect Allele: T

P-value: 1.059281*10^-6

Effect Size: 0.8849243

Effect Allele Frequency: 0.1258224

Genotyped?: Imputed

# Your code goes here
gwas1_sorted <- gwas1 %>% sort_by(gwas1$Score.pval)

top_snp <- gwas1_sorted %>% head(n = 1) 
top_snp <- top_snp[c("variant.id", "chr", "pos", "other_allele", "effect_allele", "freq", "Score.pval", "Est", "genotyped")]

top_snp
## # A tibble: 1 × 9
##   variant.id   chr      pos other_allele effect_allele  freq Score.pval   Est
##        <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>        <chr>         <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl>
## 1     542766    19 28492903 C            T             0.126 0.00000406 0.885
## # ℹ 1 more variable: genotyped <lgl>

Sina plot

The code below gives you an example of how to merge in genotypes for a variant from the results with the sample annotation file.

Edit the code below for the top SNP you just identified.

# load sample annotation file
load(paste0(shared_folder, "p8_study1_sample_info.RData"), verbose = TRUE)
## Loading objects:
##   sample.info
head(pData(sample.info))
##   sample.id    sex pop   height
## 1   HG00096   male EUR 170.5518
## 2   HG00097 female EUR 155.1560
## 3   HG00099 female EUR 157.5750
## 4   HG00100 female EUR 151.2360
## 5   HG00101   male EUR 161.9721
## 6   HG00102 female EUR 148.3681
# open SeqArray GDS file of genetic data from study 1
gds <- seqOpen(paste0(shared_folder, "p8_study1_imputed_data.gds"))
gds
## Object of class "SeqVarGDSClass"
## File: /ix1/hugen2072-2026s/p8/p8_study1_imputed_data.gds (481.4M)
## +    [  ] *
## |--+ description   [  ] *
## |--+ sample.id   { Str8 2548 LZMA_ra(7.84%), 1.6K } *
## |--+ variant.id   { Int32 647629 LZMA_ra(6.24%), 157.9K } *
## |--+ position   { Int32 647629 LZMA_ra(48.6%), 1.2M } *
## |--+ chromosome   { Str8 647629 LZMA_ra(0.03%), 489B } *
## |--+ allele   { Str8 647629 LZMA_ra(18.1%), 526.3K } *
## |--+ genotype   [  ] *
## |  |--+ data   { Bit2 2x2548x647629 LZMA_ra(4.04%), 31.8M } *
## |  |--+ extra.index   { Int32 3x0 LZMA_ra, 18B } *
## |  \--+ extra   { Int16 0 LZMA_ra, 18B }
## |--+ phase   [  ]
## |  |--+ data   { Bit1 2548x647629 LZMA_ra(0.01%), 29.5K } *
## |  |--+ extra.index   { Int32 3x0 LZMA_ra, 18B } *
## |  \--+ extra   { Bit1 0 LZMA_ra, 18B }
## |--+ annotation   [  ]
## |  |--+ id   { Str8 647629 LZMA_ra(36.2%), 2.3M } *
## |  |--+ qual   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(0.02%), 533B } *
## |  |--+ filter   { Int32,factor 647629 LZMA_ra(0.02%), 533B } *
## |  |--+ info   [  ]
## |  |  |--+ AF   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(46.2%), 1.1M } *
## |  |  |--+ MAF   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(46.0%), 1.1M } *
## |  |  |--+ AVG_CS   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(22.8%), 577.6K } *
## |  |  |--+ R2   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(53.8%), 1.3M } *
## |  |  |--+ ER2   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(15.1%), 380.9K } *
## |  |  |--+ IMPUTED   { Bit1 647629 LZMA_ra(13.6%), 10.8K } *
## |  |  \--+ TYPED   { Bit1 647629 LZMA_ra(68.1%), 53.9K } *
## |  \--+ format   [  ]
## |     |--+ DS   [  ] *
## |     |  \--+ data   { Float32 2548x647629 LZMA_ra(1.42%), 89.2M } *
## |     |--+ GP   [  ] *
## |     |  \--+ data   { Float32 2548x1942887 LZMA_ra(1.23%), 232.3M } *
## |     \--+ HDS   [  ] *
## |        \--+ data   { Float32 2548x1295258 LZMA_ra(0.95%), 119.2M } *
## \--+ sample.annotation   [  ]
# Extract genotype for one variant
#  this code just does the first variant in the results file
# Change my_chr and my_variant to match your variant of interest

my_chr <- "19" # change this to the chromosome you want (keep as character)
my_variant <- 542766 # change this to the variant.id you want (keep as numeric)

# Filter gds file to chromosome of interest
seqSetFilterChrom(gds, include=my_chr)
## # of selected variants: 30,037
# Additionally filter to variant.id of interest
seqSetFilter(gds, variant.id=my_variant, verbose=TRUE, action = "intersect")
## # of selected variants: 1
# Get dosage of variant
geno <- altDosage(gds)

# Close gds file
seqClose(gds)

# Create a data object with genotypes
geno_df <- as_tibble(geno, rownames = "sample.id")
names(geno_df)[2] <- c("genotype")

# Merge with annotation file
annot_geno <- inner_join(pData(sample.info), geno_df, by=c("sample.id"))

# Examine genotype distribution
annot_geno %>% count(genotype)
##   genotype    n
## 1        0 1950
## 2        1  555
## 3        2   43

Create a sina plot that shows the raw values of height across genotype groups at this top variant.

Comment on whether you think the genotype model you specified is appropriate for this variant or not.

Since the 0/1 genotypes are roughly the same on the y-axis and the 2 genotype is bumped up that there may be some recessiveness in the genotype. I think the overall model with gene dosages is good and the sina plot looks good, even if the 2 dosage group doesn’t have a lot of data points compared to the other groups.

# Your code goes here 
tmp <- annot_geno %>% group_by(genotype) %>% 
  summarise(sd = sd(height, na.rm = TRUE), mean = mean(height, na.rm = TRUE))

annot_geno %>% ggplot(aes(x = genotype, y = height, group = genotype))+
  geom_sina(alpha = 0.3)+
  geom_pointrange(aes(x = genotype, y = mean, ymin = mean-sd, ymax = mean+sd), data = tmp, inherit.aes = FALSE)

LocusZoom plot

Edit the code below to create a LocusZoom plot centered around the SNP you just identified. Based on this plot you created, how confident do you feel that this signal is not a false positive? Explain.

########################################
### Code you need to edit
my_chr <- "19" # change this to the chromosome you want (keep as character)
my_variant <- 542766 # change this to the variant.id you want (keep as numeric)
my_lz_window <- c(28242903, 28742903) # change to be the positions for the window you want to plot (typically +/- 250 kb around your top SNP)
#######################################


# a function to calculate LD from a gds file for a given index variant
calculateLD <- function(gdsfile, chr, variant.id, ref.var=NULL, sample.id=NULL) {
    gds <- seqOpen(gdsfile)
    seqSetFilterChrom(gds, include=chr)
    seqSetFilter(gds, variant.id=variant.id, sample.id=sample.id, verbose=TRUE, action = "intersect")
    geno <- altDosage(gds)
    seqClose(gds)

    # correlations for just ref.var, or the entire matrix?
    if (is.null(ref.var)){
        geno.ref <- geno
    } else {
        geno.ref <- geno[,as.character(ref.var)]
    }

    # drop unnecessary dimensions (if ref.var is a single variant)
    r <- drop(cor(geno, geno.ref, use="pairwise.complete.obs"))
    
    ld <- as_tibble(r^2, rownames="variant.id") %>% 
      dplyr::rename(LD = value) %>% 
      mutate(variant.id = as.numeric(variant.id)) %>% 
      dplyr::select(variant.id, LD)
    
    return(ld)

}

# Subset results file to region of interest
gwas1_locus <- 
  gwas1 %>% 
  filter(chr == as.numeric(my_chr) & pos >= my_lz_window[1] & pos <= my_lz_window[2])

# calculate LD with variant
ld <- calculateLD(gdsfile = paste0(shared_folder, "p8_study1_imputed_data.gds"),
                  chr = my_chr,
                  variant.id = gwas1_locus$variant.id,
                  ref.var=my_variant) 
## # of selected variants: 30,037
## # of selected variants: 40
# merge LD back with gwas results
gwas1_locus <- inner_join(gwas1_locus, ld %>% dplyr::select(variant.id, LD), by = "variant.id")

# create a unique SNP identifier column
gwas1_locus <- 
  gwas1_locus %>% 
  mutate(snp_id = paste(chr, pos, other_allele, effect_allele, sep=":"))

# locuszoom plot
loc <- locus(data = gwas1_locus, 
             p = "Score.pval",
             xrange = my_lz_window, 
             seqname = my_chr,
             labs = "snp_id",
             LD = "LD",
             ens_db = "EnsDb.Hsapiens.v86")
## chromosome 19, position 28242903 to 28742903
## 40 SNPs/datapoints
summary(loc)
## Index SNP 19:28492903:C:T 
## Chromosome 19, position 28,242,903 to 28,742,903
## 40 SNPs/datapoints
## 11 gene transcripts
## 9 lincRNA, 1 processed_transcript, 1 transcribed_unprocessed_pseudogene 
## Ensembl version: 86 
## Organism: Homo sapiens 
## Genome build: GRCh38
locus_plot(loc)

Based on this plot you created, how confident do you feel that this signal is not a false positive? Explain.

I am a little concerned about the signal being a false positive since there is no LD that would indicate the truth of the signal, this could be caused by an indel but still gives cause for concern. It would be best to look at variants near the signal to determine if it is an indel or if it is a false positive. Looking into MAF of the variant to check for inflation is also a good choice since the lack of LD is concerning for the signal.

Part 2 - PGS

Another study of height generated a polygenic score to predict height (https://www.pgscatalog.org/score/PGS002975/).

You have been provided with pre-harmonized data for the PGS scoring file p8_pgs002975_scores.txt and PLINK formatted data for study 1 p8_study1.bed|bim|fam.

Edit the template code below to point the output to your directory with the OUT_FOLDER variable.

Then run the code below. At the command line, load PLINK with module load plink/1.90b7.7, then run the PLINK command.

module load plink/1.90b7.7

# edit this to point to your directory 
OUT_FOLDER=/bgfs/jcarlson/hugen2072/Advanced_GWAS_project

CLASS_FOLDER=/ix1/hugen2072-2026s/p8/

plink --bfile $CLASS_FOLDER/p8_study1 \
--score  $CLASS_FOLDER/p8_pgs002975_scores.txt header sum \
--out $OUT_FOLDER/p8_study1_scored

Read the .profile file created by PLINK, which contains the values of the PGS and merge with the phenotypes from the annotation object.

# Your code goes here
p8_profile <- read_table("./p8_study1_scored.profile")
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   FID = col_double(),
##   IID = col_character(),
##   PHENO = col_double(),
##   CNT = col_double(),
##   CNT2 = col_double(),
##   SCORESUM = col_double()
## )
#merge
annot_geno <- rename(annot_geno, IID = sample.id)

mergedp8 <- left_join(annot_geno, p8_profile, by = "IID")

Part 2 - Report

How many SNPs were in the starting PGS scoring files were used here? How many variants matched the PLINK files for the imputed data?

There were originally 647629 variants in the PGS scoring file and all of those passed the QC. All of the variants matched the PLINK files for the imputed data as the genotyping rate is exactly 1.

Based on these results from above applying the PGS for height, evaluate the utility of this PGS in predicting height. Make sure you report the PGS p-value, phenotypic variance explained (both incremental and partial r2), and an effect size estimate.

This PGS is not very good at predicting height as the incremental r2 is only 0.0001 and the partial r2 is 0.00057. This means that the variance in the model is not very well explained by the PGS. The p-value for the PGS is 0.229 and the effect size estimate is 0.6277. Another reason the PGS is not very good at predicting height is that the p-value is not significant with an alpha of 0.05 indicating it is not a very good predictor of height.

# Your code goes here
pgs_full <- lm(height ~ SCORESUM + factor(sex) + pop + genotype, data = mergedp8)
  
pgs_reduced <-lm(height ~ factor(sex) + pop + genotype, data = mergedp8)

summary(pgs_full)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = height ~ SCORESUM + factor(sex) + pop + genotype, 
##     data = mergedp8)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -16.3996  -3.1263   0.0204   3.1735  13.7243 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)     168.6792     0.3252 518.639  < 2e-16 ***
## SCORESUM          0.6277     0.5217   1.203    0.229    
## factor(sex)male  12.1457     0.1823  66.611  < 2e-16 ***
## popAMR           -9.1158     0.3059 -29.799  < 2e-16 ***
## popEAS           -5.0762     0.2692 -18.855  < 2e-16 ***
## popEUR          -13.1391     0.2705 -48.576  < 2e-16 ***
## popEUR,AFR       -6.2457     4.6013  -1.357    0.175    
## popSAS           -6.9216     0.2734 -25.318  < 2e-16 ***
## genotype          0.8299     0.1944   4.268 2.04e-05 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 4.594 on 2539 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.7351, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7343 
## F-statistic: 880.9 on 8 and 2539 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
summary(pgs_reduced)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = height ~ factor(sex) + pop + genotype, data = mergedp8)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -16.4110  -3.1314   0.0308   3.2054  13.8463 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)     168.3740     0.2036 827.159  < 2e-16 ***
## factor(sex)male  12.1517     0.1823  66.663  < 2e-16 ***
## popAMR           -9.1598     0.3037 -30.156  < 2e-16 ***
## popEAS           -5.0830     0.2692 -18.883  < 2e-16 ***
## popEUR          -13.1732     0.2690 -48.969  < 2e-16 ***
## popEUR,AFR       -6.1896     4.6014  -1.345    0.179    
## popSAS           -6.9420     0.2729 -25.440  < 2e-16 ***
## genotype          0.8357     0.1944   4.299 1.78e-05 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 4.595 on 2540 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.735,  Adjusted R-squared:  0.7343 
## F-statistic:  1006 on 7 and 2540 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
0.7351-0.735
## [1] 1e-04
rsq.partial(objF = pgs_full, objR = pgs_reduced)
## $adjustment
## [1] FALSE
## 
## $variables.full
## [1] "SCORESUM"    "factor(sex)" "pop"         "genotype"   
## 
## $variables.reduced
## [1] "factor(sex)" "pop"         "genotype"   
## 
## $partial.rsq
## [1] 0.0005698307

Part 3 - File Harmonization Before Meta Analysis

Use the R package EasyQC to perform quality checks for the GWAS from project 7 and for the second GWAS of the height from the UK Biobank (which was already been run).

You can assume the genotypes in both studies have been cleaned (although we will investigate allele frequencies a little). EasyQC is primarily checking that the GWAS output isn’t nonsensical and that alleles are coded consistently between the two studies.

The following files are provided for this part:

  • p8_study1_gwas_results.txt - standardized results from the GWAS of height from project 7
  • p8_study2_gwas2_results.txt - results from a GWAS for height from the UK Biobank (positions are in hg38) (that have been downsampled for you)
  • p8_gwas1_gwas2.ecf - a configuration file for EasyQC
  • p8_snp_reference.txt - an annotation file containing population allele frequencies

The configuration file gwas1_gwas2.ecf is almost entirely set up for you. You only need to make a few changes. Copy the file gwas1_gwas2.ecf to your home directory.

  • First read through the file and the comments. The documentation (https://homepages.uni-regensburg.de/~wit59712/easyqc/EasyQC_9.0_Commands_140918_2.pdf) also explains how the program works.

  • Specify where to write the output with the --pathOut flag (near the top of the file). Replace the path with a location within your home directory.

In R, load the EasyQC package and run your configuration script with the command like EasyQC("p8_gwas1_gwas2.ecf"), supplying the path to your .ecf file. This will take a few minutes.

The output includes a log, report (with counts of the number of variants dropped for each cleaning step), some plots, some more files containing information about variants failing some of the filters, and the cleaned data (i.e., GWAS results with problematic variants removed).

Note: everything that you will need to move forward is created as output files. So, you can run the next code chunk once and then leave eval=FALSE for the chunk when you knit this file.

Also note that multiple runs of this script file does NOT overwrite the results, it names them with successful counters (e.g., CLEANED.study_1.gz CLEANED_study_1.1.gz). I recommend you only run this once, otherwise you will need to carefully edit the metal script in the next part to point to the correct files.

#/ix1/hugen2072-2026s/p8/ 

# Process the GWAS files with EasyQC
# change this to the path to your folder where you have your copy of the ecf file
EasyQC("p8_gwas1_gwas2.ecf")

Part 3 - Report

Read the cleaned individual GWAS results (CLEANED.study_1.gz and CLEANED.study_2.gz from EasyQC), and the EasyQC report file (p8_gwas1_gwas2.rep) into R.

Summarize the sample sizes for the two individual GWASs.

How many variants were in each of the two individual GWAS (after EasyQC filtering)?

According to the EasyQC report, how many variants were dropped from each?

The GWAS for study 1 had 104466 variants in and 107531 of those variants made it through the GWAS. Study 2 had 488915 variants in and 483354 of those variants made it out. This means that 3,065 variants were added to study 1(?) and 5,561 were dropped from study 2.

How many were flagged for allele frequency discrepancies, for being duplicates, or for failing some other validity check?

Study 1dropped 1 variants for invalid SE, and 44 variants for monomorph. Study 2 dropped 31 variants for invalid SE, and 2 variants for invalid beta. In the .rep file there was nothing indicating any variants dropped due to being duplicates or allele frequency duplications, but there were some reference mismatching and some recoding that happened in the data.

# Your code goes here
clean_1 <- fread("./CLEANED.study1.gz")
clean_2 <- fread("./CLEANED.study2.gz")
rep <- fread("./p8_gwas1_gwas2.rep")

rep
##    fileInShortName numVarIn numVarOut numDrop_Missing_Alleles numDrop_Missing_P
##             <char>    <int>     <int>                   <int>             <int>
## 1:          study2   488915    483354                       0                 0
## 2:          study1   104466    107531                       0                 0
##    numDrop_Missing_BETA numDrop_Missing_SE numDrop_Missing_EAF
##                   <int>              <int>               <int>
## 1:                    0                  0                   0
## 2:                    0                  0                   0
##    numDrop_Missing_N numDrop_invalid_PVAL numDrop_invalid_SE
##                <int>                <int>              <int>
## 1:                 0                    0                 31
## 2:                 0                    0                  1
##    numDrop_invalid_BETA numDrop_invalid_EAF numDrop_Monomorph
##                   <int>               <int>             <int>
## 1:                    2                   0                41
## 2:                    0                   0                 0

##                               <int>                 <int>
## 1:                                0                     0
## 2:                                0                     0

## 1:                        0                       0                       0
## 2:                        0                       0                       0
##    HA.HA.num_Recoded_SEQ HA.HA.numDrop_InvalidAlleles CPTID.from_chr_pos
##                    <int>                        <int>              <int>
## 1:                 17425                            0             488841
## 2:                  1402                            0             104465
##    CPTID.nomatch CPTID.allelemismatch numDuplicates.cptid NotInRef NotInIn
##            <int>                <int>               <int>    <int>   <int>
## 1:             0                    0                7244   405475  565184
## 2:             0                    0                   1     1402  534988
##    AA.Checked AA.StrandChange AA.AlleleMatch AA.AlleleChange AA.AlleleMismatch
##         <int>           <int>          <int>           <int>             <int>
## 1:      80231               0          75511             154              4566
## 2:     109574               0         103062               0              6512

##                  <int>               <int>                     <num>

## 2:                3067                   0                 0.9807507

## 1:               3730 360388      12.503142  0.002136       1.822568
## 2:                936   2548       1.012655  0.263700       2.799482

Part 4 - Meta Analysis

Use METAL to meta-analyze the two GWAS results.

First, read the documentation to learn how to make a script that meta-analyzes the two studies (https://genome.sph.umich.edu/wiki/METAL_Documentation).

The configuration file p8_metal_script.txt has been provided. Read through this file and use the documentation for METAL at the link provided to make sense of what options have been used in this script.

Copy this script to your folder. Edit the file to provide the paths to the EasyQC output files CLEANED.study_1.gz and CLEANED.study_2.gz.

At the command line, load metal with module load metal/2020-05-05. To run the meta-analysis use the command metal p8_metal_script.txt. Make sure that you run this from the folder that contains your copy of p8_metal_script.txt.

The output will be a log file and a text file with the suffix “.TBL”, which contains a table of meta-analysis results.

module load metal/2020-05-05
metal p8_metal_script.txt

Note that multiple runs of this script file does NOT overwrite the results, it names them with successful counters (e.g., METAANALYSIS_1.TBL, METAANALYSIS2.TBL). Be sure you read in the results file you want to by editing the code below.

Read the meta-analysis results (the .TBL file) into R and make Manhattan/Q-Q plots for the meta-analysis results. Edit the code provided below to point to the results file in your directory. You will need to turn on this chunk prior to knitting by setting EVAL=TRUE.

# Your code goes here
metal <- fread("METAANALYSIS1.TBL")

#https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/separate.html
#Used link above to learn how to seperate the marker column to align with the needs of manhattan plot

metal2 <- separate(metal, sep = ":", col = MarkerName, into = c("chr", "pos"), extra = "drop") 
metal2$chr <- as.numeric(metal2$chr)
metal2$pos <- as.numeric(metal2$pos)

metal2$snp <- metal$MarkerName

manhattan(metal2, chr = "chr", bp = "pos", p = "P-value", snp = "snp")

qq(metal2$`P-value`)

Part 4 - Report

Based on your reading of the metal script, what method of meta-analysis did we use?

The methods of the method script use standard error and effect size estimates to analyse the data across the two GWAS studies.

Using the meta-analysis results you read in above, do the following:

Make a table summarizing effect directions across the two studies

    --     -?     -+     ?-     ?+     +-     +?     ++   
    16615  18826  16438 206959 208123  16597  19253  16735

How many variants are shared across the two studies (i.e., how many have a non-missing effect and p-value in BOTH studies)?

66385 variants are shared across the two studies.

How many variants are unique to each study?

453161 variants are unique for the studies, 38079 for allele 1 and 415082 for allele 2.

What percentage of variants present in both studies have the same effect direction in both studies?

50.24% of the variants present in both studies have the same effect direction.

For the most significant variant in the meta-analysis that was present in both studies, make a forest plot that shows the two individual study effect estimates and confidence intervals. Hint: you will need to read in the cleaned files from Easy QC for each study. Comment on whether the variant’s effects seems to be homogeneous (based on the forest plot).

NOTE: Metal returns Allele1 and Allele2 and the effects and directions it reports are for Allele1. If you want the opposite allele coding, you need to multiple the effect estimate by -1.

# Your code goes here

#table summarizing effect directions across the two studies
table(metal2$Direction)
## 
##     --     -?     -+     ?-     ?+     +-     +?     ++ 
##  16615  18826  16438 206959 208123  16597  19253  16735
#how many have a non-missing effect and p-value in BOTH studies
#all columns with no ?
total <- 16615+16438+16597+16735
total
## [1] 66385
#How many variants are unique to each study?
#opp of above
total2 <- 18826+206959+208123+19253
total2
## [1] 453161
#What percentage of variants present in both studies have the same effect direction in both studies?
((16615+16735)/(16615+16438+16597+16735))*100
## [1] 50.23725
#FOREST PLOT
#For the most significant variant in the meta-analysis that was present in both studies, make a forest plot that shows the two individual study effect estimates and confidence intervals. Hint: you will need to read in the cleaned files from Easy QC for each study. Comment on whether the variant's effects seems to be homogeneous (based on the forest plot).

#commented out for the length of the knitted file
#sort_by(metal2, metal2$`P-value`)

#scrolled through until the Direction column didn't have a ?
metal2[24829,]
##       chr      pos Allele1 Allele2  Effect StdErr   P-value Direction HetISq
## 24829  20 35384250       t       c -0.0573  0.006 2.574e-21        +-   36.9
##       HetChiSq HetDf HetPVal         snp
## 24829    1.585     1   0.208 20:35384250
clean_1[POS == 35384250,]
##          cptid   CHR      POS STRAND EFFECT_ALLELE OTHER_ALLELE    EAF  BETA
##         <char> <int>    <int> <char>        <char>       <char>  <num> <num>
## 1: 20:35384250    20 35384250      +             T            C 0.5067 0.114
##        SE   PVAL     N   MAC
##     <num>  <num> <int> <num>
## 1: 0.1352 0.3993  2548  2514
clean_2[POS == 35384250,]
##          cptid   CHR      POS STRAND EFFECT_ALLELE OTHER_ALLELE    EAF     BETA
##         <char> <int>    <int> <char>        <char>       <char>  <num>    <num>
## 1: 20:35384250    20 35384250      +             T            C 0.6321 -0.05763
##          SE       PVAL      N    MAC
##       <num>      <num>  <int>  <num>
## 1: 0.001706 9.569e-250 360388 265200
meta_results <- data.frame(study = c("Study 1", "Study 2", "meta"), beta = c(0.114, -0.05763, -0.0573), se = c(0.1352, 0.001706, 0.006))

meta_results <- meta_results %>% mutate(l95 = beta - 1.96*se, u95 = beta + 1.96*se)

ggplot(meta_results, aes(y=fct_rev(study)))+
  geom_point(aes(x = beta, size = factor(study == "meta"), shape = factor(study == "meta")))+
  geom_linerange(aes(xmin = l95, xmax = u95))+
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed")+
  scale_size_manual(values = c(2,7))+
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(16,18))+
  theme_classic()+
  theme(legend.position = "none", axis.line.y = element_blank(), axis.ticks.y = element_blank(), axis.title.y = element_blank())
